title: Protein Calculator - Daily Protein Requirements
tagline: Calculate your optimal daily protein intake based on body weight, activity level, fitness goal, and age.
intro: Enter your body weight, activity level, and fitness goal to get a personalized daily protein recommendation.

error.invalid: Please enter a valid body weight and age.
error.weightZero: Body weight must be greater than zero.

result.heading: Daily Protein Requirement
result.lbmBasis: Based on {lbm} kg lean body mass ({activity}, {goal})

about.heading: About the protein calculator
about.body:
Protein is the macronutrient responsible for building, repairing, and maintaining virtually every tissue in the human body. Muscles, bones, skin, hair, enzymes, hormones, and immune antibodies are all constructed from protein's constituent amino acids. Unlike carbohydrates and fats, the body cannot store protein as a dedicated reserve, so an adequate daily supply from food is essential for normal physiology.

How much protein you need depends on several interacting factors. Body weight or, more precisely, lean body mass is the primary driver because muscle tissue is the dominant protein consumer. A 90 kg powerlifter and a 90 kg individual with high body fat have very different lean-mass bases and therefore different protein needs. Activity level amplifies requirements: resistance and endurance training increase muscle-protein turnover, raising the demand above baseline. Fitness goal matters too — someone trying to gain muscle mass needs more raw material than someone simply maintaining their current physique, while a person in a calorie deficit needs extra protein to prevent muscle loss during fat burning.

Age is a critical but often overlooked variable. After about age 50, skeletal muscle becomes progressively less sensitive to the anabolic signal of protein, a phenomenon linked to sarcopenia (age-related muscle loss). Older adults therefore need higher protein per kilogram to achieve the same muscle-protein synthetic response as younger individuals. Some research suggests adults over 65 benefit from intakes of 1.2–1.6 g/kg even for simple maintenance.

The calculator uses evidence-based activity multipliers: 0.8 g/kg for sedentary individuals (the minimum recommended by the World Health Organization), rising through 1.0 g/kg for light exercisers, 1.2 g/kg for moderate exercisers, 1.6 g/kg for very active individuals, and up to 2.0 g/kg for elite athletes. Goal adjustments then apply a further 20% increase for weight-loss (to spare muscle while in a calorie deficit) or an 80% increase for muscle building, consistent with meta-analyses on hypertrophy nutrition. An age factor of 1.1 for adults aged 50–64 and 1.2 for those 65 and over is applied on top.

When body fat percentage is provided, the calculator switches to a lean-body-mass basis. LBM = total weight × (1 − body-fat%). Since fat tissue requires virtually no protein for maintenance, LBM-based calculations are more precise, particularly for individuals at the extremes of body composition — very lean athletes or those with high body-fat percentages. For practical purposes the two methods converge for most people with average body composition.

Protein quality matters alongside quantity. Animal sources (meat, fish, eggs, dairy) supply all nine essential amino acids in proportions that closely match human muscle protein. Plant sources (legumes, grains, soy, quinoa) can deliver sufficient essential amino acids when varied throughout the day, though digestibility is generally somewhat lower, which is why some dietitians recommend a modest upward adjustment for strict plant-based diets.

Timing and distribution also influence outcomes. Research on muscle-protein synthesis suggests spreading intake across three to four meals of 25–40 g each is more effective than consuming the same total in one or two sittings. Post-exercise protein (within two hours) is particularly beneficial for recovery and adaptation. A casein-rich snack before sleep can sustain overnight muscle-protein synthesis.

Use this calculator as a starting point. Monitor your energy, strength, recovery, and body composition over four to eight weeks, then adjust intake up or down by 10–20 g per day as needed. Anyone with kidney disease or other medical conditions should discuss protein targets with a qualified healthcare provider before making significant dietary changes.

examples.heading: Protein calculator examples
examples.intro: Real-world scenarios illustrating how activity level, goal, and age shift daily protein needs.

items.note: Minimum WHO recommendation for a sedentary adult. Protein factor 0.8 g/kg.

items.input: 80 kg, very active, 28 yr, muscle building
items.note: High-frequency gym-goer targeting hypertrophy. Factor 1.6 × 1.8 = 2.88 g/kg.
Older adult with age factor 1.2 to counter sarcopenia. Factor 1.0 × 1.2 = 1.2 g/kg.

items.input: 90 kg, elite athlete, 26 yr, muscle building
items.note: Multiple training sessions daily. Factor 2.0 × 1.8 × 1.0 = 3.6 g/kg — at the upper end of evidence-based recommendations.

howto.heading: How to use the protein calculator
steps: Enter your body weight and select kg or lbs. If you know your body fat percentage, enter it for a lean-mass-based result.
Enter your age. The calculator automatically applies the appropriate age factor for adults over 50.
Choose the activity level that best reflects your average weekly exercise. Be honest — overestimating leads to more protein than you need.
Select your primary fitness goal: Weight Maintenance, Weight Loss, or Muscle Building. Each applies a different goal multiplier.
Click Calculate. Your daily protein target appears in grams per day, together with the weight basis used for the calculation.

items.q: How much protein do I need per kilogram of body weight?
items.a: It depends on your activity and goals. Sedentary adults need about 0.8 g/kg (the minimum to prevent deficiency). Recreational exercisers typically need 1.0–1.4 g/kg, while strength athletes aiming for muscle growth often target 1.6–2.2 g/kg. The calculator applies evidence-based multipliers for your specific profile.

items.q: Does protein quality matter as much as quantity?
items.a: Yes. Animal proteins (meat, fish, eggs, dairy) contain all nine essential amino acids and are highly digestible. Plant proteins (legumes, grains) can meet all essential amino acid needs when eaten in variety, but their lower digestibility means plant-based eaters may benefit from targeting the higher end of the recommended range.

items.q: Why do older adults need more protein?
items.a: After age 50, muscle tissue becomes less sensitive to the anabolic effect of protein — a phenomenon called anabolic resistance. Higher per-kilogram intake compensates for this reduced efficiency. The calculator applies a 10% increase for ages 50–64 and a 20% increase for ages 65 and over to account for sarcopenia risk.

items.q: Can I eat too much protein?
items.a: For healthy individuals, intakes up to 2.2 g/kg body weight appear safe. Very high intakes (above 3 g/kg) may cause digestive discomfort and place unnecessary stress on the kidneys in susceptible individuals. The calculator keeps recommendations within evidence-based safe ranges. Anyone with kidney disease should consult a clinician before significantly increasing protein.

items.q: Should I use total body weight or lean body mass for the calculation?
items.a: Lean body mass (LBM) is more physiologically accurate because fat tissue has essentially zero protein requirement. If you know your body fat percentage, enter it for an LBM-based result. If not, the total-weight method works well for most people with average body composition and is the standard used in most research guidelines.

items.q: How should I distribute protein intake throughout the day?
items.a: Research consistently shows that spreading protein across three to four meals of 25–40 g each optimises muscle-protein synthesis better than consuming the same total in one or two large servings. A post-workout serving within two hours of training and a slow-digesting (casein) source before sleep can further enhance recovery and overnight muscle maintenance.
